name: Labrador Retriever
notes: Prone to hip/elbow dysplasia and obesity. Healthy weight management saves significant routine-vet cost over a lifetime.

name: French Bulldog
notes: Brachycephalic — higher rates of respiratory issues, allergies, and C-section births. Insurance is often a strong fit for this breed.

name: Golden Retriever
notes: Coat requires regular grooming. Hip dysplasia common. Generally healthy and responsive to vet care.

name: German Shepherd
notes: Hip dysplasia is common. High energy requires good nutrition and preventive care.

name: Bulldog
notes: Brachycephalic with frequent respiratory, skin, and joint issues. One of the higher-cost breeds.

name: Maine Coon
notes: Long coat means real grooming costs. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) screening is recommended.

name: Persian
notes: Extensive grooming required to prevent matting. Prone to eye issues and respiratory problems.

name: Siamese
notes: Generally healthy. Low grooming needs. Minimal breed-specific health issues.

name: Ragdoll
notes: Large breed with moderate grooming needs. Generally healthy with good lifespan.

name: Chihuahua
notes: Small, generally healthy. Dental disease and patellar luxation are the most common cost drivers. Long lifespan spreads costs across more years.

name: Poodle (Standard)
notes: Hypoallergenic coat needs professional grooming every 4-8 weeks. Generally healthy compared to other large breeds.

name: Dachshund
notes: Long-bodied breed with elevated risk of intervertebral disc disease (IVDD). Surgery for severe cases can run $4,000-$8,000.

name: Rottweiler
notes: Large body, deep chest. Hip/elbow dysplasia, bone cancer risk, and bloat (GDV) are documented. Anesthesia and surgical costs scale with size.

name: Australian Shepherd
notes: Heavy double coat needs regular brushing. Watch for MDR1 gene mutation affecting drug sensitivity — ask the vet to flag.

name: Pit Bull / American Staffordshire Terrier
notes: Generally healthy; skin allergies and ACL tears are most common. Insurance and rental policies sometimes apply breed-specific rules — check before adopting.

name: Beagle
notes: Generally healthy. Watch obesity (food-driven breed) and ear infections (drop ears).

name: Boxer
notes: Cancer-prone breed. Brachycephalic features and cardiac issues (boxer cardiomyopathy) drive lifetime costs up.

name: Yorkshire Terrier
notes: Long silky coat needs grooming. Dental disease and tracheal collapse are the most common cost drivers.

name: Siberian Husky
notes: Heavy double coat (massive shedding). Generally healthy but eye conditions and hip dysplasia documented.

name: Doberman Pinscher
notes: Dilated cardiomyopathy and von Willebrand disease (bleeding disorder) are documented. Pre-purchase health screening matters.

name: Pug
notes: Brachycephalic — BOAS surgery is common. Eye injuries from prominent eyes. Skin fold infections.

name: Shih Tzu
notes: Long coat needs frequent grooming. Eye and dental issues common.

name: Cavalier King Charles Spaniel
notes: Mitral valve disease (heart) and syringomyelia (neurological) are common breed risks. Insurance is often a strong fit.

name: Boston Terrier
notes: Brachycephalic features. Eye injuries and corneal ulcers common.

name: Mastiff
notes: Hip dysplasia and bloat are documented. Short lifespan due to giant size; large food and anesthesia costs.

name: Great Dane
notes: Bloat (GDV) is a major risk — preventive gastropexy is common. Cardiac issues and very short lifespan.

name: Pomeranian
notes: Patellar luxation, dental disease, and tracheal collapse. Long-lifespan breed.

name: Border Collie
notes: Generally healthy. Hip dysplasia and Collie eye anomaly possible. High-energy lifestyle adds daycare/training costs.

name: Bernese Mountain Dog
notes: Cancer-prone breed (especially histiocytic sarcoma). Hip and elbow dysplasia. Very short lifespan.

name: Cane Corso
notes: Hip dysplasia and eyelid conditions (entropion/ectropion). Massive food and anesthesia cost lines.

name: Miniature Schnauzer
notes: Pancreatitis-prone (avoid fatty foods). Regular grooming required to maintain coat.

name: Cocker Spaniel
notes: Recurring ear infections (drop ears + heavy fur). Eye conditions. Regular grooming a must.

name: Australian Cattle Dog
notes: Healthy breed. Very high energy — many owners use daycare or hire walkers. Deafness can occur.

name: Newfoundland
notes: Hip and elbow dysplasia, cardiac issues (subaortic stenosis), drooling. Very large food and anesthesia bills.

name: Bengal
notes: Active and playful. HCM screening recommended. Some states regulate ownership of early-generation Bengals.

name: British Shorthair
notes: Generally healthy. HCM and PKD screening recommended in breeding lines.

name: Sphynx
notes: Hairless cats need weekly bathing, sun protection, and warmth. HCM screening recommended.

name: Scottish Fold
notes: Osteochondrodysplasia (joint disease) is intrinsic to the folded-ear gene. Veterinary associations have raised welfare concerns about the breed.

name: Goldendoodle
notes: Designer cross of Golden Retriever and Poodle. Non-shedding low-allergen coat needs professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Prone to hip dysplasia, ear infections, allergies, and bloat.

name: Labradoodle
notes: Cross of Labrador Retriever and Poodle. Coat ranges from wavy to curly. Hip dysplasia, progressive retinal atrophy, ear infections, and allergies are common.

name: Bernedoodle
notes: Cross of Bernese Mountain Dog and Poodle. Premium-priced designer breed. Prone to hip and elbow dysplasia, sebaceous adenitis, and bloat.

name: Cavapoo
notes: Cross of Cavalier King Charles Spaniel and Toy/Miniature Poodle. Inherits Cavalier-line cardiac risks including mitral valve disease.

name: Shiba Inu
notes: Spitz-type Japanese breed. Self-cleaning coat needs minimal grooming. Prone to allergies, luxating patella, glaucoma, and hypothyroidism.

name: Belgian Malinois
notes: Working/protection breed used by military and police. Prone to hip dysplasia, elbow dysplasia, and epilepsy. High energy requires significant training budget.

name: Havanese
notes: National dog of Cuba. Long silky coat requires daily brushing and regular professional grooming. Prone to luxating patella, hip dysplasia, mitral valve disease, and deafness.

name: Maltese
notes: Ancient toy breed. Long white coat needs daily brushing. Prone to dental disease, luxating patella, tracheal collapse, and portosystemic liver shunt.

name: Vizsla
notes: Hungarian pointing breed. Short copper coat needs minimal grooming. Prone to hip dysplasia, hypothyroidism, and lymphoma. Very active.

name: Saint Bernard
notes: Giant alpine rescue breed. Heavy double coat sheds heavily. Prone to bloat, hip and elbow dysplasia, dilated cardiomyopathy, osteosarcoma, and entropion.

name: American Shorthair
notes: The most common cat in the U.S. Robust short coat needs minimal grooming. Mostly healthy with occasional hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and hip dysplasia.

name: Savannah Cat
notes: Hybrid descended from African serval. F1-F3 generations $5,000-$25,000; F4-F5 $1,500-$3,000. Prone to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and pyruvate kinase deficiency. Legal status varies by state.

name: Russian Blue
notes: Quiet, gentle pedigree cat with distinctive blue-grey double coat. Very healthy with few documented inherited disorders. Long-lived (16-20 years typical).

name: Norwegian Forest Cat
notes: Large semi-longhair Scandinavian breed. Water-resistant double coat sheds seasonally. Prone to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, glycogen storage disease type IV, and hip dysplasia.

name: Abyssinian
notes: Ticked-coat active breed with strong East African ancestry. Prone to progressive retinal atrophy, pyruvate kinase deficiency, gingivitis, and renal amyloidosis.

name: Munchkin
notes: Short-legged cat breed from a spontaneous dominant gene mutation. Banned by some registries due to welfare concerns. Prone to lordosis and pectus excavatum.

name: Basset Hound
notes: Long back + drop ears drive IVDD and chronic ear-infection cost. Obesity-prone.

name: Bichon Frise
notes: Allergic dermatitis is extremely common. Monthly professional grooming is non-negotiable.

name: Cockapoo
notes: Cocker × Poodle cross. Low-shedding but high grooming. Ear-infection risk inherited from Cocker side.

name: German Shorthaired Pointer
notes: Very high-energy sporting breed. Bloat risk and orthopedic costs drive the bill if exercised inadequately.

name: Great Pyrenees
notes: Giant breed — anesthesia, surgery, and medication doses all scale with body weight. Bone-cancer risk meaningful.

name: Mixed Breed Dog
notes: Hybrid vigor + adoption-fee pricing make mixed-breed dogs the most cost-effective option for most households.

name: Pembroke Welsh Corgi
notes: Long back + heavy shedding. IVDD, hip dysplasia, and degenerative myelopathy are breed-specific risks.

name: West Highland White Terrier
notes: Atopic dermatitis is the famous Westie issue. Westie lung disease (idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis) is a documented breed-specific risk.

name: Devon Rex
notes: Wavy short coat, very low shedding, intensely social. HCM and congenital myasthenia gravis are breed-specific risks.

name: Domestic Shorthair
notes: Mixed-genetics non-pedigree cat — the most common cat type in U.S. households. Hybrid vigor + adoption pricing make this the most cost-effective cat option.

name: Exotic Shorthair
notes: Brachycephalic Persian-derivative with a short coat. Eye discharge, dental crowding, PKD risk inherited from Persian line.

name: Siberian Cat
notes: Heavy triple coat, generally robust, marketed as lower-allergen due to reduced Fel d 1 production in many individuals.

name: Rhodesian Ridgeback
notes: Distinctive ridge along the spine. Dermoid sinus (congenital), hip dysplasia, and bloat are the breed-specific costs to plan for.

name: Soft Coated Wheaten Terrier
notes: Low-shedding silky coat needs daily brushing. Protein-losing nephropathy (PLN) and protein-losing enteropathy (PLE) are documented breed-specific risks.

name: Burmese
notes: Highly social, dog-like cat. Hypokalemic polymyopathy (DNA testable), diabetes, and HCM are documented breed risks. Among the longest-lived cat breeds.

name: Oriental Shorthair
notes: Siamese body type in 300+ colors. Extremely vocal and social. Amyloidosis (kidney/liver) and anesthesia sensitivity are breed-specific concerns.

name: Birman
notes: Color-pointed semi-longhair with distinctive white "gloves" on all four paws. HCM and congenital hypotrichosis are documented breed risks. Coat lacks undercoat — minimal matting.
